name: Aurora
description: Aurora, the Roman goddess of dawn, is a figure of ethereal beauty and significant symbolic weight within the Roman pantheon. Equivalent to the Greek Eos, she is responsible for opening the gates of the sky each morning, announcing the arrival of her brother Sol (the sun). Her name, derived from the Latin word for dawn, perfectly encapsulates her role as the herald of daylight. Aurora is often depicted as a winged goddess, either riding a chariot drawn by swift horses or flying across the sky, scattering dew and roses as she goes. Her association with the dawn imbues her with themes of renewal, hope, and the cyclical nature of time. She is a powerful reminder of the constant return of light after darkness, and the promise of a fresh start each day. The Roman poets frequently invoked Aurora, celebrating her beauty and her vital role in the natural order, solidifying her place as a central figure in their mythology.
subtitle: Goddess of the Dawn

keyMyths.title: Aurora and Tithonus
keyMyths.description: One of the most well-known myths associated with Aurora involves her love for the mortal Tithonus. Captivated by his beauty, she abducted him and begged Jupiter to grant him immortality. Jupiter granted her wish, but Aurora forgot to ask for eternal youth. As Tithonus aged, he became increasingly frail and withered, eventually turning into a cicada. This myth serves as a cautionary tale about the importance of considering all aspects when seeking immortality and the bittersweet nature of eternal life without eternal youth. Aurora’s grief over Tithonus is often cited as the reason for the dewy tears she sheds each morning.

keyMyths.title: Aurora and Cephalus
keyMyths.description: Aurora also fell in love with the mortal Cephalus, a handsome hunter. She abducted him in an attempt to make him her lover. However, Cephalus remained devoted to his wife, Procris, and resisted Aurora's advances. Eventually, Aurora, frustrated by his loyalty, sent him back to Procris, planting seeds of doubt in his mind about her faithfulness. This led to a tragic chain of events where Cephalus accidentally killed Procris with a javelin, highlighting the destructive potential of jealousy and the meddling of the gods in mortal affairs.

keyMyths.title: Aurora and Memnon
keyMyths.description: Aurora was the mother of Memnon, a Trojan prince who fought valiantly in the Trojan War. When Memnon was killed by Achilles, Aurora was overcome with grief. Her tears, shed each morning for her fallen son, are said to be the morning dew. This myth connects Aurora to the themes of mourning, maternal love, and the consequences of war. The Colossi of Memnon in Egypt were believed to sing each morning as Aurora greeted her son's spirit with the dawn.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of mythological stories, offering detailed accounts of Aurora's relationships with Tithonus and other mortals.

sources.author: Hyginus
sources.description: A collection of summaries of classical myths, providing concise accounts of Aurora's role in various narratives.

sources.author: Quintus Smyrnaeus
sources.description: An epic poem continuing the story of the Trojan War, including Aurora's grief over the death of her son Memnon.
